question: ∫01(xex)2=? \int_{0}^{1} \left ( x e^{x} \right )^{2} = ? ∫01(xex)2=?
explanation: Let I=∫01xex2dx \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx} I=∫01xex2dx Put x2=t⇒2xdx=dt \mathrm{x}^{2}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{xdx}=\mathrm{dt} x2=t⇒2xdx=dt As x→0,t→o \mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0, \mathrm{t} \rightarrow \mathrm{o} x→0,t→o and as x→1,t→1 \mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1, \mathrm{t} \rightarrow 1 x→1,t→1, ∴I=12∫01etdt⇒12∫etdt=12et=F(t) \begin{array}{l} \therefore \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{t}) \end{array} ∴I=21∫01etdt⇒21∫etdt=21et=F(t) By second fundamental theorem of calculus, we obtain I=F(1)−F(0)=12e−12e0=12(e−1) \begin{array}{l} I=F(1)-F(0) \\ =\frac{1}{2} e-\frac{1}{2} e^{0} \\ =\frac{1}{2}(e-1) \end{array} I=F(1)−F(0)=21e−21e0=21(e−1)

explanationLatex: $Solve: cos⁡4xdx=(cos⁡2x)2={12(1+cos⁡2x)}2=14{1+2cos⁡2x+cos⁡22x˙}=14{1+2cos⁡2x+12(1+cos⁡4x)}=14[1+2cos⁡2x+12+12cos⁡4x]=14[32+2cos⁡2x+12cos⁡4x]∴cos⁡4xdx=∫14(32+2cos⁡2x+12cos⁡4x)dx=14(32x+2⋅12sin⁡2x+12⋅14sin⁡4x)+c=14(32x+sin⁡2x+18sin⁡4x)+c (Ans.) \begin{aligned} & \cos ^{4} x d x=\left(\cos ^{2} x\right)^{2}=\left\{\frac{1}{2}(1+\cos 2 x)\right\}^{2} \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left\{1+2 \cos 2 x+\cos ^{2} 2 \dot{x}\right\} \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left\{1+2 \cos 2 x+\frac{1}{2}(1+\cos 4 x)\right\} \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left[1+2 \cos 2 x+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \cos 4 x\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{3}{2}+2 \cos 2 x+\frac{1}{2} \cos 4 x\right] \\ \therefore \quad & \cos { }^{4} x d x \\ & =\int \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3}{2}+2 \cos 2 x+\frac{1}{2} \cos 4 x\right) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3}{2} x+2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4} \sin 4 x\right)+c \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3}{2} x+\sin 2 x+\frac{1}{8} \sin 4 x\right)+c \text { (Ans.) } \end{aligned} ∴cos4xdx=(cos2x)2={21(1+cos2x)}2=41{1+2cos2x+cos22x˙}=41{1+2cos2x+21(1+cos4x)}=41[1+2cos2x+21+21cos4x]=41[23+2cos2x+21cos4x]cos4xdx=∫41(23+2cos2x+21cos4x)dx=41(23x+2⋅21sin2x+21⋅41sin4x)+c=41(23x+sin2x+81sin4x)+c (Ans.)$

question: ∫tan⁡−1(x5)dx=? \int \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right) d x = ?∫tan−1(5x)dx=?
explanation: Solve: ∫tan⁡−1(x5)dx \left.\int \tan ^{-1} \frac{(x}{5}\right) d x ∫tan−15(x)dx=tan⁡−1(x5)∫dx−∫[ddx{tan⁡−1(x5)}∫dx]dx =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right) \int d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}\left\{\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)\right\} \int d x\right] d x =tan−1(5x)∫dx−∫[dxd{tan−1(5x)}∫dx]dx =xtan⁡−1(x5)−∫1/51+(x5)2xdx=xtan⁡−1(x5)−∫xdx25+x2=xtan⁡−1(x5)−12∫(0+2x)dx25+x2=xtan⁡−1(x5)−12ln⁡(25+x2)+c \begin{array}{l} =x \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)-\int \frac{1 / 5}{1+\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)^{2}} x d x \\ =x \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)-\int \frac{x d x}{25+x^{2}} \\ =x \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(0+2 x) d x}{25+x^{2}} \\ =x \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(25+x^{2}\right)+c \end{array} =xtan−1(5x)−∫1+(5x)21/5xdx=xtan−1(5x)−∫25+x2xdx=xtan−1(5x)−21∫25+x2(0+2x)dx=xtan−1(5x)−21ln(25+x2)+c

question: ∫0π/2sin⁡2xcos⁡xdx=?\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \cos x d x \quad = ?∫0π/2sin2xcosxdx=?
explanation: Solve: ∫0π/2sin⁡2xcos⁡xdx=∫0π/212(sin⁡3x+sin⁡x)dx=12[−13cos⁡3x−cos⁡x]0π/2=12(−13cos⁡3π2,−cos⁡π2+13cos⁡0+cos⁡0)=12[−13(cos⁡3π2−cos⁡0)−(cos⁡π2−cos⁡0)]=12[−13(0−1)−(0−1)]=12(13+1)=23 \begin{array}{l} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \cos x d x \quad \\ =\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{2}(\sin 3 x+\sin x) d x \\ =\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{1}{3} \cos 3 x-\cos x\right]_{0}^{\pi / 2} \\ =\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{1}{3} \cos 3 \frac{\pi}{2},-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{1}{3} \cos 0+\cos 0\right) \\ =\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{1}{3}\left(\cos 3 \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos 0\right)-\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos 0\right)\right] \\ =\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{1}{3}(0-1)-(0-1)\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3}+1\right)=\frac{2}{3} \end{array} ∫0π/2sin2xcosxdx=∫0π/221(sin3x+sinx)dx=21[−31cos3x−cosx]0π/2=21(−31cos32π,−cos2π+31cos0+cos0)=21[−31(cos32π−cos0)−(cos2π−cos0)]=21[−31(0−1)−(0−1)]=21(31+1)=32

explanation: ∫tan⁡xsin⁡x⋅cos⁡xdx=∫tan⁡xsin⁡xcos⁡x⋅cos⁡2xdx=∫tan⁡xtan⁡xcos⁡2x=∫sec⁡2xtan⁡x=2tan⁡x[∫f′(x)f(x)dx=2f(x)] \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\sqrt{\tan x}}{\sin x \cdot \cos x} d x \\ = & \int \frac{\sqrt{\tan x}}{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x} \cdot \cos ^{2} x} d x \\ = & \int \frac{\sqrt{\tan x}}{\tan x \cos ^{2} x} \\ = & \int \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{\sqrt{\tan x}} \\ = & 2 \sqrt{\tan x}\left[\int \frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)} d x=2 \sqrt{f(x)}\right]\end{aligned} ====∫sinx⋅cosxtanxdx∫cosxsinx⋅cos2xtanxdx∫tanxcos2xtanx∫tanxsec2x2tanx[∫f(x)f′(x)dx=2f(x)]
